title: Calculation contract
description: Metaschema for shared/calculations/<discipline>/<calc>.json files. Each calculation declares an `executor` — `inline` (LLM applies the formula directly, safe for closed-form arithmetic) or `tool` (the runtime invokes a named Python tool because LLM math diverges from validated code, e.g. iteration, table lookups, cross-validation).

executor.description: inline = LLM applies formula; tool = runtime calls a registered Python tool

tool_name.description: Required when executor=tool. Must appear in shared/calculations/REQUIRED_TOOLS.json. Namespace prefix `calc.` is reserved for tool calls.

output.description: Output schema — either a single named result or an object of named results

formula.description: Required when executor=inline. Plain-text formula the LLM applies.

formula_reference.description: Standard citation (e.g. 'BS 7430:2011 §§ 9.5–9.7', 'CIBSE SLL Code for Lighting 2012')

implementation_note.description: Why this executor mode was chosen. For tool-executor: explain WHY LLM math is risky (iteration, table lookup, cross-validation, etc.).

runtime_contract.description: Required when executor=tool. Names the request/response shape the runtime expects.

sub_calculations.description: Optional. For composite calcs (e.g. lumen method) that bundle multiple closed-form steps. Each sub-calc may carry its own formula. The top-level `formula` is the headline output formula.

$executor_decision_checklist._note: Quick checklist for picking executor when authoring a new calc. Mark as `tool` if ANY of the following is true; otherwise mark as `inline`.
rules: Calc involves iteration to convergence (e.g. electrode-array parallel resistance)
Calc requires a lookup against a standards table (e.g. cable rating tables, Zs max tables)
Calc has been measured to diverge >5% from a validated Python reference on representative inputs
Calc requires cross-validation across multiple outputs (e.g. cable size, voltage drop, fault level coordinated together)
Calc's output drives a safety boundary where precision below ±1% matters (e.g. CPC adiabatic check)
